title: Workstation Security — Unattended Unlocked Sessions in Clinical Pods
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the HIPAA Security programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with HIPAA Security control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: HIPAA Security §164.310(b) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that HIPAA Security §164.310(b) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.10.28: Workstation Security — Unattended Unlocked Sessions in Clinical Pods.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.10.28' running on WinEventLog:Security 4801 (unlock), 4800 (lock), index=epic sourcetype=epic:audit from same Workstation_ID, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Workstation Security — Unattended Unlocked Sessions in Clini' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper HIPAA Security control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the HIPAA Security control objective.

spl:
index=windows sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" EventCode IN ("4800","4801") earliest=-7d
| eval action=if(EventCode="4800","lock","unlock")
| transaction WorkstationName startswith=eval(action="unlock") endswith=eval(action="lock") maxspan=8h
| eval idle_during=if(duration>3600 AND eventcount<3,1,0)
| where idle_during=1
| table WorkstationName, duration, user, _time
description: Correlates long gaps between workstation unlock events in nursing stations with active Hyperspace sessions to reduce shoulder-surfing and unauthorized viewing risks.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for HIPAA Security compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes epic, windows and sourcetypes WinEventLog:Security, epic:audit`. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Deploy only on designated clinical OU to limit noise; (2) pair with physical security rounds checklist; (3) educate on clean desk + lock policy. Indexes required: `index=epic`, `index=windows`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security`, `sourcetype=epic:audit``.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Break-the-glass emergency access** — Clinicians exercising documented emergency access procedures to ePHI during patient safety events generate elevated access alerts that are legitimate under the HIPAA treatment, payment, and operations (TPO) exception.

2. **Health information exchange (HIE) activity** — Automated ePHI transmissions through approved health information exchanges and interoperability interfaces produce cross-boundary data flows that are authorised under business associate agreements.

3. **Clinical research data extraction** — IRB-approved research protocols involve de-identified or limited data set extractions that trigger data access monitoring rules but are covered by the approved research data use agreement.

4. **System backup and archival processes** — Scheduled ePHI backup operations, media rotation, and long-term archival procedures generate bulk data access events that are operationally required and documented in the information lifecycle policy.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `hipaa_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved break-glass event IDs, HIE partner identifiers, IRB protocol numbers, and scheduled backup job identifiers. The correlation search filters against this lookup and suppresses matches where `hipaa_exception_approved=true`.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase app ID 742) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `windows` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `§164.310(b)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that HIPAA Security §164.310(b) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.10.28: Workstation Security — Unattended Unlocked Sessions in Clinical Pods." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase app ID 742), landing events in `windows` under `WinEventLog:Security`. Security channel alone produces 2000-8000 events per domain-joined host per day depending on audit policy (~2-8 GB/host/month). Domain controllers generate 10x more.

Configure the input through Splunk_TA_windows/local/inputs.conf on each Universal Forwarder. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[WinEventLog://Security]
disabled = 0
index = windows
renderXml = true
current_only = 0
evt_resolve_ad_obj = 1
checkpointInterval = 5
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `EventCode, Account_Name, Account_Domain, Logon_Type, Source_Network_Address, Workstation_Name, Process_Name, TargetUserName, Subject_Account_Name`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=windows earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** Deploy only on designated clinical OU to limit noise. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** pair with physical security rounds checklist. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** educate on clean desk + lock policy. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Workstation Security — Unattended Unlocked Sessions in Clinical Pods," the aggregation summarises access activity — the control is about WHO accessed WHAT, so the key fields in the aggregate map directly to 'principal' and 'resource' for the compliance narrative.

This search uses `| transaction` to group events that share a correlation identifier into a single result row. Transactions are analytically powerful but operationally expensive: Splunk must buffer events in memory until the transaction closes, which caps the practical event volume (default `maxevents=1000` per transaction, `maxopentxn=10000` concurrent transactions).

Scale warning: for compliance searches that must cover multi-day spans with potentially tens of thousands of open transactions, `| transaction` will either truncate or OOM. Prefer `| stats earliest(...) latest(...)` grouped by the same correlation ID — it produces the same analytical result with no memory cap and 5-20x better performance.

When `| transaction` is the right choice: when you need the event sequence preserved (e.g. detecting a specific ordering of events like 'login -> elevate -> access -> logout'), when you need `maxpause` or `maxspan` constraints between events, or when you need `startswith`/`endswith` markers. If you only need min/max of a field across the group, always use `| stats`.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.10.28: Workstation Security — Unattended Unlocked Sessions in Clini]
cron_schedule = 0 2 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -7d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.10.28,reg=HIPAA_Security
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `WorkstationName`, `action`, `duration`, `idle_during`, `user`. Run:

```spl
index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, WorkstationName, action, duration, idle_during, user
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `duration`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(duration) as p50 perc90(duration) as p90 perc99(duration) as p99 max(duration) as hi
```

The current threshold `3600` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.10.28: HIPAA Security evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Histogram (`session durations`):** Reveals the distribution shape of the measured metric. Useful for calibrating thresholds — the shape tells you whether the current threshold sits on the tail or in the body of the distribution.

**Panel 2 — Drill-through table (`worst workstations`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**HIPAA Security-specific failure modes:**

• Minimum-necessary principle: access-anomaly detections (excessive chart views, unexpected departments) must NOT flag clinicians accessing records for treatment purposes — §164.502(b) explicitly permits this. Join detections against the clinician's current department and patient's active encounter to avoid generating thousands of false positives on ED doctors seeing admitted patients.

• Break-glass and emergency access (§164.312(a)(2)(ii)): emergency accounts will legitimately bypass normal access controls during downtime events. Maintain a lookup `breakglass_declarations.csv` written by the clinical informatics on-call; correlate every break-glass access against a declared event — un-declared emergency access is the finding.

• Audit log retention (§164.312(b)): HIPAA requires 6 years of audit log retention, longer than most Splunk index default settings. Verify `frozenTimePeriodInSecs >= 189216000` for every index named in an HIPAA UC's search, and that the archive tier (S3, cold storage) is provisioned.

• Covered vs non-covered entity scope: if the search unintentionally pulls data from a non-covered business unit (e.g. a research arm not operating as a HIPAA Covered Entity), findings are noise. Filter using the facility/cost-center lookup that the Privacy Officer maintains, not by index name alone.
grandmaExplanation: We correlate long gaps between workstation unlock events in nursing stations with active Hyperspace sessions to reduce shoulder-surfing and unauthorized viewing risks. HIPAA is about keeping health records private and secure when we use them in care and operations.
